text: This video is going to highlight my technique for a fully hand stoned anastomosis in a robotically assisted left hemicolectomy.
My concern with these cases has always been that a stapled anastomosis really just didn't lay right And the crotch of the anastomosis always lay over the ligament of trites.
That really always bothered me, not just from an aesthetic standpoint, but just technically. I didn't like the way that looked and the way that functioned.
So I've always wanted to do this anastomosis hand-sewn end-to-end, truly end-to-end. And really the only way to do that is to do it fully hand sewn with no staple line.
And obviously the robot really is pretty much the only way to do that.

The surgeon performs a standard left hemicolectomy, excising a tumor located near the splenic flexure. Both ends of the colon are stapled, and the excised specimen is placed aside, leaving two stapled ends visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon sews the staple lines together, preparing the site for a hand-sewn two-layered anastomosis.
The surgeon begins the hand-sewn anastomosis using a double-ended V-lock suture, three-oh absorbable, looped together, visible in the surgical field.
What I like about the starting out with the staple line suture together is that it lines up your anastomosis very well.
The surgeon creates an enterotomy on both sides of the stapled ends, preparing for the anastomosis. The video is shown at double speed to demonstrate the procedure.
The surgeon places the first suture through the corner of the stapled end, threading it through the loop and pulling the loop down to secure the initial stitch.
The surgeon uses a third hand to hold the staple lines while starting the first stitch with a double-ended V-lock suture, aligning the tissue for the anastomosis.
The surgeon uses a double-ended V-lock suture to secure the anastomosis, ensuring full thickness coverage across both the proximal and distal ends of the enterotomy.
The surgeon holds up the end of the tissue and places two stitches before pulling the suture all the way through, demonstrating a technique to efficiently manage the anastomosis.

The surgeon continues to suture across the full thickness of the tissue, moving down to the corner. Upon reaching the corner, the surgeon positions it on both the distal and proximal sides, pulls the suture through, and initiates the anterior lumbar technique.
The surgeon adjusts the stitching technique from outside-in to inside-out, aiming for a full-thickness stitch visible through the tissue layers.
The surgeon positions the limber on both the proximal and distal ends of the surgical site, preparing to start stitching on the opposite side, ensuring precise placement of each stitch.
The surgeon lines up the posterior suture line by holding the other end of the double-ended stitch, preparing to grab a second stitch for further suturing.
The surgeon places the second anterior layer of stitches, using a third arm to hold and align the stitches evenly on both the proximal and distal ends.

The surgeon places a loop and uses the third arm to manage the stitches, ensuring the correct needle is used among the three present. The stitch is run back to verify accuracy before proceeding to the corner.
The surgeon demonstrates full-thickness stitches being placed across the defect at the end of the posterior layer, ensuring the sutures penetrate completely through the tissue.

The surgeon demonstrates the full-thickness suturing of the two lumens, carefully limiting the inclusion of mucosa to prevent pouting in the center. The lembert suture is initiated on the anterior side superiorly and is run straight down to meet the previous stitch.
The surgeon carefully tightens each stitch in the closure, ensuring the sutures are taut but not overly tight to avoid creating a purse-string effect that could constrict the lumen.

I like this limber technique for the anterior surface because it really brings it together nicely. Again, prevents the mucosa from pouting out. It's very, very nice. Very standard hand-sewn technique for open surgery and really adapted readily to the robot.
The surgeon uses a third robotic arm to hold the stitch at the top end, aligning the two lumens evenly across the surgical field, ensuring equal progress on both the proximal and distal ends.
The surgeon opens the lumen, revealing its interior, while performing hand-switching maneuvers using robotic assistance.
The surgeon uses needle drivers to carefully grasp the edge of the colon wall while suturing, avoiding excessive pressure to prevent damage to the bowel tissue.
The surgeon demonstrates the Lembert technique, visibly bringing the anterior surface of the colon together with precise tension to prevent mucosal protrusion.
The surgeon completes the Lembert suturing technique along the bowel, transitioning to simple stitches for the final section. The back wall is avoided by lifting the tissue during stitching.
The surgeon lifts the distal end of the colon and aligns it straight across, preparing to tie the sutures for added security.
The surgeon grabs the anterior second layer of the colon and brings it down, taking seromuscular bites with the needle drivers.
The surgeon aligns the tissue at the staple line, bringing down the posterior first layer to ensure precise alignment and prevent skipping spots during the suturing process.
I'm done. I think this is a really easy reproducible technique that can really be done by anyone with basic robotic skills and produces a really nice anastomosis. Thank you for watching.
